title: Subdiffusion equation with Cattaneo effect
authors: Tadeusz Kosztołowicz, Aldona Dutkiewicz, Katarzyna D. Lewandowska
abstract: The ordinary subdiffusion equation, with a fractional time derivative of at most first order, describes a process in which the propagation velocity of diffusing molecules is unlimited. To avoid this non-physical property different forms of the Cattaneo subdiffusion equation have been proposed. We define the Cattaneo effect as a delay of the ordinary subdiffusion flux activation by a random time. By incorporating this effect into the flux equation we get a Cattaneo--type subdiffusion equation (CTSE). We consider a subdiffusion process in which the Cattaneo effect is generated by the time-delay probability distribution controlled by the Mittag-Leffler function. Then, CTSE differs from the ordinary subdiffusion equation by a term with a fractional time derivative, whose order can be independent of the subdiffusion exponent. The influence of the Cattaneo effect on the solutions to the CTSE is discussed. We show that the process described by CTSE is subdiffusion in the entire time domain even though the temporal evolution of the mean square displacement of diffusing particle in the short-time limit is typical for superdiffusion. The delay in the flux activation in the subdiffusion equation should also cause a flux delay in a boundary condition. As an example, we study subdiffusion with the Cattaneo effect in a system with a partially absorbing wall at which the Robin boundary condition is assumed. We also propose a method for experimentally identifying the Cattaneo effect in a subdiffusive system.
summary: This paper proposes a modified subdiffusion equation that incorporates a Cattaneo-type delay to prevent the non-physical infinite propagation velocity found in standard fractional diffusion models. It shows that while the effect significantly alters short-time dynamics, the system remains subdiffusive in the long-time limit. The work also emphasizes the necessity of including this delay effect in boundary conditions for physical consistency.
main_problem: Addressing the non-physical infinite propagation velocity in ordinary subdiffusion equations by introducing a Cattaneo-type effect that incorporates a random time delay in flux activation.
main_result: The authors derive a Cattaneo-type subdiffusion equation (CTSE) that ensures a finite propagation velocity and demonstrate that the Cattaneo effect modifies short-time dynamics without inducing true superdiffusion.
method: The study uses Laplace transforms, Riemann-Liouville and Caputo fractional derivatives, and series expansions to solve the fractional differential equations and analyze asymptotic limits.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional homogeneous system describing subdiffusive processes (such as transport in viscoelastic media or porous media) where the flux is regulated by a Mittag-Leffler-controlled delay distribution.
key_observables: Green's function (probability density), Mean Square Displacement (MSD), and the probability density of particle absorption at a partially absorbing wall.
important_parameters: Subdiffusion exponent (alpha), Cattaneo effect parameter (kappa), delay strength (tau), diffusion coefficient (D), and wall absorption parameter (beta).
main_assumptions: The diffusion parameters are assumed to be time-independent and independent of concentration; the Cattaneo effect is assumed to be present in both the governing equation and the boundary conditions for physical consistency.
figures_summary: Figures compare Green's functions and MSD evolution with and without the Cattaneo effect across short and long time limits, and illustrate the impact of including the effect in boundary conditions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of infinite velocity, derives the CTSE using Laplace transforms, analyzes the short-time and long-time asymptotic behaviors of the Green's function and MSD, investigates boundary conditions at a partially absorbing wall, and proposes an experimental identification method.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Plots of Green’s functions for short times given in the legend. Lines with filled symbols represent Green’s functions Eq. (30) for the subdiffusion equation with the Cattaneo effect, for 𝛼= 0.5, 𝜅= 0.9, 𝜏= 10, and 𝐷= 10. Lines with empty symbols are GF Eq. (34) plots for the subdiffusion equation without this effect, for 𝛼= 0.5 and 𝐷= 10.
Figure 2: Relative changes of the GF 𝑃𝑅Eq. (35) for the functions presented in Fig. 1, for parameters given in the legend.

Figure 5: Time evolution of MSD for 𝛼= 0.5 and 𝜅= 0.9. The line with filled symbols is the plot of 𝜎2 𝑆𝑇(𝑡) for short times Eq. (39), the line with open symbols represents 𝜎2 𝐿𝑇(𝑡) for long times Eq. (40). The calculations have been made taking into account the 50 leading terms in the series defining the functions. The boundary between short and long times is at the common point of both lines at 𝑡≈220. The line without symbols represents the time evolution of the MSD for the equation without the Cattaneo effect Eq. (37).
Figure 6: Plots of the time evolution of MSD for 𝛼= 0.9 and 𝜅= 0.5, the description is as for Fig. 5.
